question_text: What is the primary source of the light we see coming from the Moon?
options: A) The Moon generates its own light through internal heat
B) The Moon reflects sunlight
C) The Moon reflects light from Earth's atmosphere
D) The Moon is powered by cosmic radiation
explanation: The sources state that the Moon does not shine under its own power but merely glows with reflected sunlight. (Source:)

question_text: According to the text, what is the actual cause of the Moon's phases?
options: A) The shadow of the Earth falling on the Moon
B) Changes in the Moon's distance from the Sun
C) The motion of the Moon relative to the Sun as seen from Earth
D) The Moon's rotation speed changing throughout the month
explanation: The phases are explained by the motion of the Moon relative to the Sun; how much of the illuminated face we see depends on the angle the Sun makes with the Moon from our vantage point. (Source:)

question_text: At any given position in its orbit, how much of the Moon's total surface is illuminated by the Sun?
options: A) The entire Moon
B) Exactly half of the Moon
C) Only the portion visible from Earth
D) A percentage that varies from 0% to 100% depending on the phase
explanation: In every position of its orbit, the Moon is half illuminated and half dark, just as a ball in sunlight should be. (Source:)

question_text: When is the Moon in its 'New' phase?
options: A) When it is opposite the Sun in the sky
B) When it is at a 90-degree angle to the Sun
C) When it is in the same general direction as the Sun
D) When it is visible only at midnight
explanation: The Moon is new when it is in the same general direction in the sky as the Sun, meaning its illuminated side is turned away from Earth. (Source:)

question_text: Approximately how many degrees does the Moon move eastward in the sky each day?
options: A) 1 degree
B) 5 degrees
C) 12 degrees
D) 30 degrees
explanation: Because it takes about 30 days to orbit Earth's 360-degree circle, the Moon moves approximately 12 degrees in the sky each day. (Source:)

question_text: Which phase describes the Moon when it rises at sunset and sets at sunrise?
options: A) New Moon
B) First Quarter
C) Full Moon
D) Third Quarter
explanation: When the Moon is full, it is opposite the Sun in the sky, rising at sunset and setting at sunrise. (Source:)

question_text: What have statistical tests revealed regarding the 'lunacy' hypothesis and human behavior?
options: A) Homicides increase by 20% during full moons
B) Hospital emergencies are more frequent during the new moon
C) There is no correlation between human behavior and the phases of the Moon
D) Human behavior is only affected by the Moon in cultures without electric lights
explanation: Statistical tests of thousands of records from hospitals and police files do not reveal any correlation of human behavior with the phases of the Moon. (Source:)

question_text: What is the length of the 'solar month,' the time interval in which the lunar phases repeat?
options: A) 24.0 days
B) 27.3 days
C) 29.5 days
D) 30.0 days
explanation: The solar month, or the time interval in which the phases repeat (e.g., full to full), is 29.5306 days. (Source:)

question_text: Why does the Moon always keep the same face turned toward Earth?
options: A) It does not rotate on its axis at all
B) It rotates on its axis in the same amount of time it takes to revolve around Earth
C) Earth's shadow locks the Moon's rotation
D) The Moon is too far away for us to see it rotate
explanation: This is due to synchronous rotation: the Moon rotates on its axis in exactly the same time that it takes to revolve about Earth. (Source:)

question_text: Which statement best describes the 'dark side' of the Moon?
options: A) It is the side that never receives any sunlight
B) It is a specific side that is always dark because the Moon does not rotate
C) It is a misunderstanding; the Sun rises and sets on all sides of the Moon
D) It is only dark during the full moon phase
explanation: The term 'dark side' is a misunderstanding; because the Moon rotates, the Sun rises and sets on all sides, and the back side is dark no more frequently than the front side. (Source:)
